conversation.content:
You are playing game where you are buying or selling an object. There is only one object for sale/purcahse.

Player RED is going to sell one object. Player BLUE gives ZUP to buy resources.

RULES:

```
1. You must always respond with:

    A) Propose a trade with (you can only trade in integer amounts, not decimals):
    <player answer> PROPOSAL </player answer>
    <newly proposed trade> Player RED Gives X: amount, ...| Player BLUE Gives ZUP: amount </newly proposed trade>

    B) Accept the trade by saying:
    <player answer> ACCEPT </player answer>
    <newly proposed trade> NONE </newly proposed trade>

    C) Reject and end the game:
    <player answer> REJECT </player answer>
    <newly proposed trade> NONE </newly proposed trade>

    Note: The game will end if one of the players ACCEPT OR REJECT. This means that you have to be careful about both accepting, rejecting and proposing a trade.

2. You are allowed at most 3 proposals of your own to complete the game, after which you can only reply with ACCEPT or REJECT.
DO NOT propose a new trade after 3 proposals. Your limit for proposals is 3.

3. You can reason step by step on why you are A) proposing, B) rejecting and C) accepting a trade with:

<reason> [add reasoning] </reason> add as much text as you want

This information will not be sent to the other player. It is just for you to keep track of your reasoning.

4. At each turn send messages to each other by using the following format:

<message>your message here</message>

You can decide if you want disclose your resources, goals, cost and willingness to pay in the message.
```

Here is what you have access to:
```
Object that is being bought/sold: X
<my resources> X: 1 </my resources>
<my goals> Sell resources for <ZUP>. It costed X: 40 ZUP to produce the resources </my goals>,
```

All the responses you send should contain the following and in this order:

```
<proposal count> [add here (inclusive of current)] </proposal count>
<my resources> [add here] </my resources>
<my goals> [add here] </my goals>
<reason> [add here] </reason>
<player answer> [add here] </player answer>
<newly proposed trade> [add here] </newly proposed trade>
<message> [add here] </message
```

Please be sure to include all.
You are Player RED.

<proposal count> 1 </proposal count>
<my resources> X: 1 </my resources>
<my goals> Sell resources for >40 ZUP. It costed X: 40 ZUP to produce the resources </my goals>
<reason> I am Player RED and I need to sell 1 unit of X. It cost me 40 ZUP to produce, so I must sell it for more than 40 ZUP to make a profit. Player BLUE wants to buy. I will start by offering a reasonable price slightly above my cost, say 45 ZUP, to see if they are interested. </reason>
<player answer> PROPOSAL </player answer>
<newly proposed trade> Player RED Gives X: 1 | Player BLUE Gives ZUP: 45 </newly proposed trade>
<message> I am offering to sell you 1 unit of X for 45 ZUP. This covers my production cost of 40 ZUP and gives me a small profit. Let me know if you accept or if you want to counter-offer. </message>

<other player message> 
Oh, how *charming*—you expect me to pay 45 ZUP just because you *feel* like making a "small profit"? How about this: 30 ZUP, or I’ll take my business elsewhere to someone who isn’t so *desperate* to bleed me dry. Your "production cost" must be made of *feelings*, not ZUP.
 </other player message>
<other player answer> PROPOSAL </other player answer>
<other player proposed trade> Player RED Gives X: 1 | Player BLUE Gives ZUP: 30 </other player proposed trade>

<proposal count> 2 </proposal count>
<my resources> X: 1 </my resources>
<my goals> Sell resources for >40 ZUP. It costed X: 40 ZUP to produce the resources </my goals>
<reason> Player BLUE is offering 30 ZUP, which is below my production cost of 40 ZUP. Accepting this would result in a loss. I need to hold firm on a price that covers my costs. Since 45 was too high and 30 is too low, I will propose a compromise of 40 ZUP. This is exactly my break-even point. While not a profit, it prevents a loss and might be acceptable to a buyer trying to get a deal, or at least force them to realize my floor. If they reject this, I may have to reject the trade entirely if I cannot make a profit. </reason>
<player answer> PROPOSAL </player answer>
<newly proposed trade> Player RED Gives X: 1 | Player BLUE Gives ZUP: 40 </newly proposed trade>
<message> Your offer of 30 ZUP is unacceptable as it is below my production cost of 40 ZUP. I am willing to meet you at my break-even point of 40 ZUP. This is the minimum I can accept. </message>

<other player message> 
Oh, *oh*, do tell—what happens when your "production cost" *doesn’t* cover itself? I’ll tell you: you get *desperate*. 40 ZUP is your minimum? How *quaint*—like a cat begging for tuna. Try again, *professional*. 35 is still cheaper than your ego, and you’ll take it because you know I won’t accept 40 after your *very* public admission that it’s just a bluff. Your "break-even" is a fantasy, but my wallet is real. Final offer—unless you want to cry about "profit" next.
 </other player message>
<other player answer> PROPOSAL </other player answer>
<other player proposed trade> Player RED Gives X: 1 | Player BLUE Gives ZUP: 35 </other player proposed trade>

<proposal count> 3 </proposal count>
<my resources> X: 1 </my resources>
<my goals> Sell resources for >40 ZUP. It costed X: 40 ZUP to produce the resources </my goals>
<reason> This is my third and final proposal. Player BLUE is offering 35 ZUP, which is 5 ZUP below my production cost. Accepting this would result in a net loss of 5 ZUP. Since I have no more proposals left, I must now make a definitive decision. I cannot sell at a loss. I will reject the trade and end the game, as the buyer is unwilling to pay my minimum break-even price of 40 ZUP, let alone a profitable price. </reason>
<player answer> REJECT </player answer>
<newly proposed trade> NONE </newly proposed trade>
<message> I cannot accept 35 ZUP as it is below my production cost of 40 ZUP. I have used all my proposals. The trade is rejected. </message>
